one_liner: A 29-year-old male combat veteran presents with 8 months of recurrent nightmares, hypervigilance, emotional numbing, and avoidance of crowded places following deployment to a combat zone.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient is a combat veteran who returned from deployment 10 months ago. During deployment, he witnessed an IED explosion that killed two members of his unit. He reports the onset of symptoms approximately 2 months after returning home. He has recurrent, vivid nightmares about the explosion 4-5 nights per week, waking up drenched in sweat and screaming. He experiences intrusive flashbacks triggered by loud noises (fireworks, car backfiring, slamming doors), during which he feels as though he is 'back in the combat zone.' He avoids conversations about the military and has stopped watching the news. He refuses to drive near construction zones or attend Fourth of July celebrations. He describes emotional numbness and feeling 'detached' from his wife and children. He has lost interest in activities he previously enjoyed (hunting, fishing). He is hypervigilant, always sitting with his back to the wall, constantly scanning for threats. He has an exaggerated startle response. He reports difficulty falling asleep (initial insomnia) and irritability with angry outbursts. He endorses difficulty concentrating at his new civilian job. He denies suicidal ideation but admits 'it might be easier if I wasn't here.' PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist) score is 62 (probable PTSD, threshold ≥31-33).
stage2.pmh: Mild traumatic brain injury during deployment (concussion, resolved). Chronic low back pain from military service.
stage2.medications: Ibuprofen 600 mg PRN for back pain. Melatonin 5 mg nightly (minimally effective for sleep).
stage2.family_history: Father with alcohol use disorder. Mother with depression. Paternal grandfather was a Vietnam veteran who 'was never the same after the war' (likely undiagnosed PTSD). No family history of psychosis.
stage2.social_history: Recently separated from active duty military, working as a warehouse supervisor. Married with two children (ages 3 and 5). Reports marital strain due to irritability and emotional withdrawal. Has been drinking 4-6 beers nightly to 'help sleep and stop thinking.' Denies other substance use. Previously social and active, now avoids gatherings. Gun owner (firearms are in a locked safe, wife has the key since his symptoms started). No legal history.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Muscular man appearing stated age, sitting rigidly with back to the wall, scanning the room**; Guarded. HEENT: **Normocephalic**; **Old, healed scar on left forehead (from blast injury)**; **Pupils equal and reactive**. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, mildly elevated blood pressure. Lungs: Clear bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Musculoskeletal: **Mild paraspinal tenderness in lumbar area**; Neurological: Cranial nerves intact, normal motor and sensory exam, normal reflexes; **Mental Status Exam: Appearance — clean, casual clothes, appears tense**; **Behavior — hypervigilant, watches door, flinches at sudden noise in hallway (exaggerated startle)**; Speech — normal rate, terse, measured; **Mood — 'on edge**; **' Affect — restricted, constricted, with brief flashes of anger when discussing the incident**; **Thought process — linear, goal-directed**; **Thought content — intrusive trauma memories, avoidance, passive SI ('might be easier if I wasn't here') but denies plan or intent, contracts for safety**; **Perception — flashbacks (dissociative, triggered by loud noises), no hallucinations**; Cognition — oriented x4, mildly impaired concentration; **Insight — fair (acknowledges symptoms are related to combat experience)**; **Judgment — fair (firearms secured)**.

lab_results.result: Na 141, K 4.3, Cl 103, CO2 25, BUN 16, Cr 1.0, Glucose 98, AST 38, ALT 42, Alk Phos 72, Albumin 4.2
lab_results.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated AST/ALT likely related to nightly alcohol use; no severe hepatic dysfunction
Elevated GGT consistent with regular alcohol consumption, supporting concern for alcohol use disorder
Not acutely intoxicated at time of evaluation
Mild vitamin D deficiency common in patients with reduced outdoor activity and social withdrawal
Normal testosterone; low testosterone can contribute to fatigue, low mood, and irritability in male veterans
Mild cognitive deficits may be related to PTSD-related hyperarousal/concentration difficulty or residual mild TBI effects

imaging_results.result: No acute intracranial abnormality. No residual evidence of prior traumatic injury. No mass or hemorrhage.

key_tests: PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist for DSM-5) — score 62 (well above threshold of 31-33 for probable PTSD)
Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) — passive SI without plan or intent
AUDIT (Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test) — to screen for comorbid alcohol use disorder
acceptable_tests: MoCA or neurocognitive screening (history of TBI)

stage5.final_clue: The patient meets all DSM-5 criteria for PTSD following a Criterion A traumatic event (witnessing death of comrades in combat).

debrief.classic_presentation: A trauma-exposed individual presenting with four symptom clusters persisting >1 month after a Criterion A event: (1) Intrusion — flashbacks, nightmares, intrusive memories; (2) Avoidance — of trauma reminders (internal and external); (3) Negative cognitions and mood — emotional numbing, detachment, guilt, diminished interest; (4) Hyperarousal — hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, insomnia, irritability, concentration difficulty.
debrief.pathophysiology: PTSD involves fear conditioning with failure of extinction. The amygdala is hyperreactive (enhanced fear response), while the medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) shows decreased activity (impaired fear extinction and emotional regulation). The hippocampus shows reduced volume and impaired contextualization of traumatic memories, leading to overgeneralized fear responses. HPA axis dysregulation results in paradoxically low cortisol with enhanced negative feedback. Noradrenergic hyperactivity from the locus coeruleus contributes to hyperarousal and nightmares. Epigenetic modifications and neuroplasticity changes maintain the disorder chronically.
debrief.treatment_overview: First-line psychotherapy: Prolonged Exposure (PE) therapy and Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT) have the strongest evidence. EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing) is also effective. First-line pharmacotherapy: SSRIs (sertraline and paroxetine are FDA-approved for PTSD). Prazosin (alpha-1 blocker) is effective for trauma-related nightmares. Avoid benzodiazepines (may worsen PTSD and impair fear extinction). Address comorbid alcohol use disorder (common in 30-50% of PTSD patients). Safety planning for passive SI. Firearm safety counseling is essential.
high_yield_pearls: DSM-5 PTSD requires a Criterion A traumatic event plus symptoms from all four clusters (intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, hyperarousal) lasting >1 month with functional impairment.
PTSD has high comorbidity with alcohol/substance use disorder (30-50%), major depression (50%), and TBI in military populations.
Prazosin is the most evidence-based treatment for PTSD-related nightmares — it blocks noradrenergic activation during REM sleep.
Benzodiazepines are CONTRAINDICATED in PTSD — they impair fear extinction learning, worsen outcomes, and increase substance use risk.
Firearm access is a critical safety concern in veterans with PTSD — always ask about firearms and encourage safe storage (lethal means restriction).
The with dissociative subtype specifier (depersonalization/derealization) identifies patients who may need different therapeutic approaches.
sources: DSM-5-TR: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition, Text Revision (2022)
VA/DoD Clinical Practice Guideline for PTSD (2023)
Raskind MA et al. — Prazosin for PTSD nightmares. NEJM 2013

after_stage2_more_likely: PTSD — all four symptom clusters present following Criterion A combat trauma, duration >1 month, significant functional impairment
Comorbid alcohol use disorder — nightly drinking of 4-6 beers as self-medication for PTSD symptoms
after_stage2_less_likely: Acute stress disorder — symptoms have persisted well beyond 1 month
Adjustment disorder — severity and symptom pattern meet full PTSD criteria
TBI sequelae — prior mild TBI resolved clinically, current symptoms are trauma-response pattern
after_labs_top3: PTSD — PCL-5 score 62, meets all DSM-5 criteria, no organic cause identified; elevated GGT supports comorbid alcohol misuse
Major depressive disorder (comorbid) — anhedonia, concentration difficulty, sleep disturbance, and passive SI overlap with depression; should be screened with PHQ-9
Alcohol use disorder (comorbid) — elevated GGT and AST/ALT with nightly heavy drinking; requires concurrent treatment
